aliases: Ketchup fried rice in egg omelette

english: Ketchup fried rice in egg omelette

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for omurice; 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002809) is the closest defensible parent class. Omurice is a Japanese dish of ketchup-seasoned fried rice wrapped in a thin egg omelette.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Fried rice) is the closest defensible match. No specific omurice code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for omurice or Japanese fried rice dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of omurice is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as commonly served in Japanese restaurants and school canteens in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Omurice — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Omurice (cr_id F05100099). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for fried rice with egg (USDA SR Legacy #21038) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for omurice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using white rice as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~64–72 for white rice per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the egg omelette wrapper, mixed vegetables, and ketchup, which collectively lower the effective GI slightly. A conservative midpoint of 65 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 21.95 × 2.5) / 100 = 35.7 ≈ 35.8 GL units for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 220 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 550 mg sodium, representing about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit omurice to one serving and request reduced ketchup and no added soy sauce or salt during preparation to keep sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base carries a medium-to-high GI (~65); however, dietary fibre at 4.34 g/100 g and protein at 6.5 g/100 g from egg and chicken help moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit omurice to one standard serving (~200–250 g), pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response, as the white rice base contributes a significant glycaemic load despite the moderating effect of egg protein and dietary fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~1.8 g/100 g from egg and butter; sodium at 220 mg/100 g; dietary fibre at 4.34 g/100 g provides a partial cardioprotective offset.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume omurice in moderation, request preparation with minimal butter and reduced ketchup to lower saturated fat and sodium, and rely on the dish's fibre content as a partial but insufficient standalone cardioprotective measure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Egg provides vitamin D and protein; onion contributes quercetin (anti-inflammatory polyphenol); saturated fat is low at ~1.8 g/100 g; trans fat is absent.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Omurice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, but those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor their response to tomato-based ketchup, and butter content should be minimised to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Omurice (オムライス, omuraisu) is a popular Japanese Western-style dish consisting of ketchup-seasoned fried rice — typically made with chicken, onion, and mixed vegetables — wrapped or topped with a thin, soft egg omelette. The name is a portmanteau of 'omelette' and 'rice'. The dish originated in Japan in the early 20th century as part of the yōshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) tradition and has since become a staple in Japanese family restaurants, school canteens, and casual dining across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The fried rice interior is moist and mildly sweet-savoury from the ketchup, while the egg exterior is silky and tender. Nutritionally, omurice provides 159 kcal per 100 g with 6.5 g protein, 5.0 g fat, and 22.0 g carbohydrate. Notably, dietary fibre is high at 4.34 g/100 g for a rice-based dish, contributed by onion and mixed vegetables. Sodium is moderate at 220 mg/100 g. The dish has a medium glycaemic index (~65) due to the white rice base, moderated by egg protein and vegetable fibre. It is suitable for most healthy adults in standard portions but requires portion control and sodium management for individuals with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. The egg omelette wrapper contributes cholesterol and saturated fat, while the ketchup adds sugar and sodium. Omurice is commonly served as a lunch or dinner dish and is a frequent item in Singapore school canteen menus.
common_user_queries: オムライス カロリー

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100099) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by dish composition. Potassium value is an estimate from HPB input metadata. All other -1 fields reflect genuine data unavailability in approved sources.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with egg (#21038) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish. This is an approximate value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for omurice not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in this preparation based on ingredient profile (no hydrogenated fats); consistent with USDA SR Legacy fried rice data. 4. potassium_mg (180 mg/100 g) is an estimate carried forward from the input record metadata; not directly confirmed in HPB database entry — treat as approximate. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate using white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct omurice GI measurement exists in approved sources. 6. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as omurice contains egg and chicken and is neither vegetarian nor plant-based. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for omurice in these systems as of 2024.
